paper_title: Evolution of the Bug River Valley during the Holocene in the environs of Janów Podlaski (Eastern Poland) in the light of malacological analysis of oxbow lake deposits

methods.description: Process for collecting sediment samples from bore-holes, handling them, and pooling them based on geological layers for subsequent malacological analysis.

steps.description: The malacological analyses included 33 samples taken from the Woroblin bore-hole at 5 cm intervals, covering a fragment of the profile at the depths of 1.5 to 3.5 m.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect sediment samples from Woroblin bore-hole at specified depth intervals

steps.description: Three of the samples contained only unidentifiable shell pieces.
steps.searchable_summary: Identify samples with only unidentifiable shell pieces
outputs: list of samples with only unidentifiable shells

steps.description: Because of the small volume of the samples and relatively poor malacofauna, the samples within the layers distinguished on the basis of the geological studies were pooled.
steps.searchable_summary: Pool samples within geological layers based on volume and malacofauna richness

steps.description: The number of samples was therefore limited to nine. Each of them covered an interval of 10-30 cm, closely corresponding to the above-described lithological units (Fig. 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Document characteristics of pooled samples (interval, correspondence to lithology)

steps.description: Fifteen samples from the bore-hole at Zaczopki were collected from the 1.55-2.30 m interval.
steps.searchable_summary: Collect sediment samples from Zaczopki bore-hole at specified depth interval

steps.description: After pooling, on the basis of the principle described above, the malacological analysis covered eight samples (Fig. 2).
steps.searchable_summary: Pool samples from Zaczopki based on the same principle as Woroblin

methods.description: Application of standard malacological analysis methods to prepared sediment samples to identify and quantify mollusc remains, distinguish ecological groups, and compile malacological spectra.

steps.description: The malacological analysis employed standard methods described by LOZEK (1964), ALEXANDROWICZ (1987) and ALEXANDROWICZ & ALEXAN-DROWICZ (2011)
steps.searchable_summary: Process pooled sediment samples using standard malacological analysis techniques

steps.description: which allowed to distinguish ecological groups characteristic of the analysed malacofauna.
steps.searchable_summary: Distinguish ecological groups based on identified mollusc species

steps.description: The proportion of species in particular samples enabled compilation of the malacological spectra for individuals, which provided a basis for interpretation.
steps.searchable_summary: Compile malacological spectra showing species proportions per sample

methods.description: Interpretation of malacological analysis results using two-component and three-component diagrams to infer environmental conditions and faunal changes over time.

steps.description: Additional data pertaining to the environmental conditions where deposits were formed, were obtained from two-component diagrams illustrating the relationship between the ecological groups
steps.searchable_summary: Create and interpret two-component diagrams of ecological group relationships

steps.description: and also three-component diagrams presenting the various stages in the development and changes in the fauna.
steps.searchable_summary: Create and interpret three-component diagrams of faunal development and change

methods.description: Calculation of the Bithynia-index (Bi) based on the shell:opercula ratio of Bithynia tentaculata to infer characteristics of the water body (open littoral vs. reed bed).

steps.description: The Bithynia-index (Bi) (STEENBERG 1917, ALEXAN-DROWICZ 1987, 1999a, b, 2007 which represents the shell:opercula ratio of Bithynia tentaculata was also calculated.
steps.searchable_summary: Calculate the Bithynia-index (Bi) as the ratio of shells to opercula for Bithynia tentaculata

steps.description: The shell predominance is characteristic of open littoral zones of lakes or other permanent water bodies, whereas the predominance of opercula is typical of reed bed zones.
steps.searchable_summary: Interpret Bithynia-index value based on shell vs opercula predominance
outputs: interpretation of water body characteristics (e.g., open littoral, reed bed)
